content: I'm finally back from my Eastern Europe trip and trying to get back into my routine. I miss the morning markets in Budapest already.
Welcome back! What was your favorite morning market find during your travels?
Definitely the fresh langos in Budapest - those fried dough treats with garlic and cheese. I've been trying to recreate them at home.
That sounds delicious! How's the home brewing going? Did you pick up any beer inspirations on your trip?
I just bottled a Czech-style pilsner. The beer scene in Prague was incredible - so many small breweries to explore.
I bet that was amazing. How are you readjusting to your interior design work after the sabbatical?
It's been challenging. I have so many new ideas from the European architecture I saw, but my commercial clients want safe, traditional designs.
That's a common struggle. Have you considered incorporating some of those Eastern European elements into your woodworking projects?
Actually, yes! I'm working on a chess set with Baltic-inspired patterns. The knights are my favorite so far.
That sounds beautiful. Do you play chess yourself or is it more of a design exercise?
I play occasionally with my brother. We had a tournament in a small town in Romania that was unforgettable.
What a unique experience! How's your Thai cooking coming along? Did you try any new techniques while traveling?
I've been perfecting my pad kra pao. The spice markets in Bangkok taught me so much about balancing flavors.
That's impressive. Do you document your culinary experiments on your travel blog?
I do! I'm actually planning a post about how travel influences my design work and cooking. The connections are fascinating.
I'd love to read that when it's published. How do you organize your creative projects now that you're back?
I've set up a new system - mornings for design work, afternoons for my hobbies, and evenings for cooking experiments.
That sounds like a well-balanced routine. How's the calligraphy practice fitting into all of this?
